title: TPU v1 · Building a chip-level verification environment with UVM: reusable Agents for array, memory, and instruction interfaces
oneLine: This case applies UVM methodology by wrapping TPU's three interface classes—the systolic array, Unified Buffer/accumulators, and host CISC commands—into reusable UVM Agents, each containing sequencer → driver → monitor. An env assembles them at chip level while each test selects scenarios and configuration knobs. Constrained-random stimulus, functional coverage, and self-checking against a Reference Model all run inside this standard framework, allowing reuse across blocks and projects.

Golden Model; mismatches fail</text></g></svg>

legend.name: uvm_test (top-level scenario)
legend.desc: The highest verification layer does not touch DUT signals. Through the factory and config_db, it configures the env, chooses a smoke, full-array, UB stress, or instruction-boundary scenario, sets random seeds, and selects sequences. Changing tests changes only the test class while preserving env structure—the UVM separation of structure from stimulus intent.

legend.name: uvm_env (assembly container)
legend.desc: Instantiates Agents and Scoreboards and connects them through TLM ports into a chip-level environment. Environments can nest: a block-level env may contain one Agent, while a chip-level env combines array, memory, and instruction Agents. The same Agent is reusable at both levels.

legend.name: Agent = Sequencer → Driver → Monitor (reusable block)
legend.desc: UVM's central reuse unit: one Agent per interface. The Sequencer creates constrained-random transactions; the Driver translates transactions into pin-level DUT timing; the Monitor passively reconstructs interface traffic and feeds functional coverage. An active Agent drives and observes, while a passive Agent only observes a previously verified sub-block. TPU's three interfaces use three structurally similar Agents.

legend.name: Monitor + covergroup (sampling and coverage)
legend.desc: The Monitor never drives; it broadcasts observed transactions to the Scoreboard and functional coverage. Covergroups sample meaningful points such as array fill depth, UB bank hits, and instruction type/length combinations, measuring whether planned scenarios occurred.

legend.name: Scoreboard (self-check against Reference Model)
legend.desc: Collects transactions from all three Monitors and compares them cycle by cycle or result by result with expectations from a high-level Reference/Golden Model. TensorFlow/XLA golden results can provide expected behavior for a TPU-like design. Any mismatch fails automatically; checking remains centralized.

steps.cap: ① The top-level test configures and builds the env through config_db. The test decides which scenario and randomization to run; the env determines how the environment is assembled—intent and structure remain separate.

② One Agent contains three components: Sequencer emits constrained-random transactions → Driver converts them to DUT pin timing → Monitor passively samples the interface. This is UVM's smallest reusable unit.
③ Give TPU's three interface classes structurally similar Agents: array, UB/accumulator memory, and host CISC instruction. Only their protocol layers differ; the chip-level env assembles all three.

④ The three Monitors send transactions to the Scoreboard for Reference Model comparison, while covergroups sample the same observations. Stimulus, checking, and coverage close the loop inside one framework.
⑤ Reuse the same Agents/env from block to chip level and across projects. A Reference Model, including TF/XLA golden results, supplies expectations for every comparison—UVM's principal value.

before: Write the verification plan before any code: list DUT features such as systolic-array fill/drain, parallel reads across UB banks, 4 MiB accumulator read-modify-write, and CISC decode/boundaries. For every feature, define stimulus, coverage, and pass criteria. The UVM environment implements this plan.

Define the Reference Model: choose the high-level behavior or TensorFlow/XLA golden output that determines expected behavior for each interface and the whole chip. Establish one authoritative correctness oracle for the Scoreboard.

Choose Agent boundaries and reuse granularity: partition by physical DUT interface—array, UB/accumulator memory, and host CISC instruction—and define each uvm_sequence_item's fields and protocol so the same Agent runs at block and chip level.

Define stimulus and constraints: use directed tests for reset and boundaries and constrained random for breadth. Constrain instruction distributions, UB address ranges, and 256×256 array limits so random generation remains legal and meaningful.

Define completion criteria (coverage closure): before implementation, quantify completion as functional coverage + code coverage targets + mandatory regression + 0 unresolved bugs. These metrics drive convergence.
during: Verify one Agent per interface before chip-level assembly: bring up each seqr→drv→mon Agent in a block-level env, then combine the verified array, memory, and instruction Agents in one chip-level env. This bottom-up approach keeps failures diagnosable.

Driver translates, Sequencer generates, Monitor observes—never mix roles: the Driver maps abstract transactions to pins but does not decide traffic; the Sequencer selects traffic but never touches pins; the Monitor passively samples and never drives. This division creates reuse across scenarios.

Keep stimulus, checking, and coverage inside the framework: use sequences for stimulus, Scoreboard + Reference Model for checks, and Monitor covergroups for coverage. Centralized components are reusable and regression-visible; ad hoc $display checks are not.

Use factory + config_db for configurability: the test injects active/passive mode, Agent enablement, random seeds, and selected sequences through config_db; the env does not hard-code them. One test-class change can move from smoke to full-load stress without structural edits.

Make self-checking automatic: the Reference Model predicts every stimulus and the Scoreboard reports uvm_error/uvm_fatal on mismatch. Waveforms are for debug only; the environment decides pass/fail so thousands of random tests can run unattended.

Scope note: UVM Sequencers, Drivers, Monitors, Scoreboards, constrained random, and coverage are industry-standard methodology. TPU v1's private environment and coverage metrics are not public. The documented array, UB/accumulator, and CISC interfaces are used only as the DUT context; covergroup and comparison snippets are illustrative.

result.label: Environment hierarchy
result.value: uvm_test → uvm_env → uvm_agent{seqr→drv→mon} → Scoreboard (four standard levels)

result.label: Three Agents for three interfaces
result.value: Array Agent (systolic array) / Mem Agent (UB + accumulators) / Instr Agent (CISC), structurally reusable

result.label: Constrained random (illustrative)
result.value: class instr_item extends uvm_sequence_item; rand bit[3:0] op; constraint c{ op inside {LOAD_W,MATMUL,...}; } (illustrative, not TPU source)

result.label: Functional coverage (illustrative)
result.value: covergroup cg @(mon.clk); cp_op: coverpoint tr.op; cp_len: coverpoint tr.len; cross cp_op,cp_len; endgroup (attached to Monitor)

result.label: Self-check/assertion (illustrative)
result.value: Scoreboard: compare(dut_tr, ref_model.predict(stim)); protocol SVA: assert property(@(posedge clk) req |-> ##[1:N] ack);

result.label: Reference Model
result.value: High-level behavior / TensorFlow·XLA golden results; Scoreboard uses it for correctness

after: Drive coverage convergence: after regression, compare functional covergroups + code coverage with the plan. Add directed tests or adjust random constraints for each coverage hole until closure.

Build an unattended regression suite: collect directed and constrained-random tests across multiple seeds; the Scoreboard automatically decides pass/fail. Rerun after every RTL change to prevent regressions.
Track the bug-convergence curve: monitor new-defect rate as it falls toward zero. Add a reproducer for every fix and continue until the 0-unresolved-bug signoff criterion is met.

Reuse and hand off methodology assets: retain verified Agents, sequences, and Scoreboards as reusable IP. Insert block-level envs into the chip-level env and future projects, and submit coverage/regression/bug-status evidence for signoff.

Release to synthesis only after signoff: functional verification must meet coverage, all-green regression, and zero-bug gates before RTL enters synthesis. Any downstream functional change returns to this environment for regression.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the systolic array, Unified Buffer, accumulators, and CISC interface are documented architectural facts; UVM Sequencers/Drivers/Monitors/Scoreboards, constrained random, functional coverage, and Reference Model checking are standard verification practices, and diagram snippets are illustrative rather than private TPU verification disclosure)
